thermostat.title: Set efficient heating and cooling temperatures
thermostat.recommendation: Use heating around 18–20°C in winter and cooling around 25–27°C in summer. Close internal doors and only condition rooms being used.
thermostat.evidence_note: Australian Government guidance states that each degree of extra heating or cooling can affect energy use by about 5–10%.

shorter_showers.title: Reduce shower time where practical
shorter_showers.recommendation: Use a shower timer and reduce long showers. Start with a realistic goal rather than forcing an extreme change.
shorter_showers.evidence_note: Water heating is commonly 15–30% of household energy use in Australia.

efficient_showerhead.title: Use a water-efficient showerhead
efficient_showerhead.recommendation: Install or request a water-efficient showerhead where suitable. This reduces hot-water demand without requiring major building work.
efficient_showerhead.evidence_note: Shower flow rates affect hot-water demand; WELS-rated products support water efficiency.

leds.title: Replace old frequently used bulbs with LEDs
leds.recommendation: Start with living rooms, kitchens, hallways, bedrooms, and outdoor lights used often.
leds.evidence_note: LEDs use about 75% less energy than halogen bulbs and last longer.

draught_sealing.title: Seal obvious draughts around doors and windows
draught_sealing.recommendation: Use door snakes, weather strips, and simple renter-friendly sealing where practical.
draught_sealing.evidence_note: Draught sealing reduces wasted heating and cooling demand.

curtains.title: Use curtains and blinds strategically
curtains.recommendation: Close curtains during hot summer afternoons and cold winter nights. Open them when outdoor conditions help comfort.
curtains.evidence_note: Curtains and blinds help manage summer heat gain and winter heat loss.

standby.title: Turn off unused appliances at the wall or use smart power boards
standby.recommendation: Focus on entertainment units, chargers, computers, gaming consoles, and rarely used devices.
standby.evidence_note: Smart power strips can reduce unnecessary standby energy waste.

insulation_owner.title: Check ceiling or roof insulation
insulation_owner.recommendation: If you own the home, check ceiling or roof insulation before investing in more expensive upgrades.
insulation_owner.evidence_note: Insulation helps reduce heat transfer and heating/cooling demand.

insulation_renter.title: Raise insulation or draught issues with the landlord/property manager
insulation_renter.recommendation: If renting, document cold/hot rooms, draughts, or poor insulation indicators and discuss options with the property manager.
insulation_renter.evidence_note: Tenure-sensitive advice is needed so renters are not given unrealistic owner-only actions.

solar_after_efficiency.title: Consider solar after basic efficiency issues are fixed
solar_after_efficiency.recommendation: Solar can be valuable, but fixing energy waste first improves the value of any later solar investment.
solar_after_efficiency.evidence_note: Whole-home energy planning should consider demand reduction before major technology upgrades.
